_meta.authoringNotes: Distinct from umbrella hypotony pages. Content cross-checked against AAO BCSC Glaucoma 2023-2024, Costa VP 2007 hypotony review, Wright MM 2010 flat AC management, EGS Terminology Guidelines 4th ed. Spaeth grading per Spaeth 1971. Bleb leak management per AGIS and TVT trial complication data.

h1Title: Flat Anterior Chamber with Hypotony: Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Flat anterior chamber with hypotony is a sight-threatening complication in which the space between the cornea and iris collapses while intraocular pressure (IOP) drops below 6 mmHg. It occurs most often within 72 hours of glaucoma filtration surgery (trabeculectomy or tube shunt) due to wound leak, overfiltration, or cyclodialysis cleft. Roughly 4-15% of trabeculectomies develop a shallow or flat anterior chamber in the early postoperative period. When the iris and cornea touch (Spaeth grade 3-4), corneal endothelial cell loss accelerates after 7 days, peripheral anterior synechiae form, and cataract progression and choroidal effusion may follow. Rapid identification of the mechanism and targeted reformation of the chamber preserve vision in 70-90% of cases.

keyStats.prevalence: 4-15% of trabeculectomies in the first 5 postoperative days (Costa 2007; AGIS data)
keyStats.demographics: Higher in elderly, high myopes, nanophthalmic eyes, and after combined cataract-trabeculectomy
keyStats.avgAge: Most cases occur in adults age 55-80 who have undergone glaucoma surgery
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 40,000-80,000 events per year globally given approximately 500,000 trabeculectomies performed annually

definition: Flat anterior chamber with hypotony (ICD-10: H44.43) is a postsurgical or post-traumatic condition characterized by severe shallowing of the anterior chamber with iris-cornea contact and an intraocular pressure below 6 mmHg, or below 6.5 mmHg with associated vision loss. The anterior chamber is graded by the Spaeth system from grade 0 (normal depth) to grade 4 (lens-cornea touch); flat AC refers to Spaeth grade 3 (peripheral iris-cornea touch with central AC preserved) or grade 4 (complete iris and lens apposition to the cornea). The condition arises when aqueous humor outflow exceeds production. Common mechanisms include external loss through a wound leak after filtration surgery (Seidel positive), excessive transscleral filtration through an overfiltering bleb, internal aqueous diversion into the suprachoroidal space via a cyclodialysis cleft (traumatic or iatrogenic), tube-shunt overdrainage, and reduced aqueous production from ciliary body shutdown in uveitis, retinal detachment, or pharmacologic suppression. The condition is distinct from primary hypotony (no surgical or traumatic trigger) and from the broader umbrella of ocular hypotony in which the chamber may remain deep. Management is shared between glaucoma, cornea, and retina subspecialists depending on mechanism and duration.

typesClassification.description: External aqueous loss through a conjunctival or scleral wound. Most common 24-72 hours after trabeculectomy. Confirmed by fluorescein streaming under cobalt-blue light. Accounts for roughly 50-60% of early postoperative cases.
Excessive transscleral aqueous flow through a thin or cystic bleb. Seidel negative. Bleb is large and avascular. Common after antimetabolite (mitomycin C, 5-fluorouracil) use.

Separation of the ciliary body from the scleral spur, creating a direct route from the AC to the suprachoroidal space. May be traumatic (blunt injury) or iatrogenic (during cataract or glaucoma surgery). Diagnosed on gonioscopy or ultrasound biomicroscopy (UBM).

Glaucoma drainage device (Ahmed, Baerveldt, Molteno) allows excessive aqueous egress, usually before fibrous encapsulation of the plate. Often associated with shallow AC, choroidal effusion, and hypotony maculopathy.

Reduced aqueous secretion from chronic uveitis, retinal detachment with proliferative vitreoretinopathy, prolonged use of aqueous suppressants, or cycliotic membranes. The chamber may shallow in advanced cases.

primarySymptoms: Blurred or fluctuating vision developing within hours to days after eye surgery or trauma, often described as a sudden drop in clarity that does not improve with blinking.
Mild to moderate dull aching in the operative eye, occasionally with a foreign body sensation if a wound leak is present.
Photopsias (brief flashes of light) due to traction on the retina or choroid from low intraocular pressure.
Watery discharge or constant tearing in cases of conjunctival wound leak, with the bleb appearing flat or deflated to the patient.
Visible bulging of the bleb or transparent fluid track on the eye surface during careful inspection in a mirror.
Halos around lights and decreased contrast sensitivity when corneal edema develops secondary to iris-cornea touch.
Distortion of central vision (metamorphopsia) from hypotony maculopathy with choroidal folds, a hallmark of chronic ocular hypotony.
Eye redness centered on the conjunctival incision, often more localized than postoperative inflammation alone would explain.
Increased sensitivity to bright light (photophobia) from associated anterior chamber inflammation and corneal endothelial stress.
Sensation of pressure or fullness in advanced cases with kissing choroidal effusions, sometimes accompanied by a shadow in the peripheral vision.
earlyWarningSigns: Bleb appearing larger, thinner, or more transparent than at the previous postoperative visit
First-day-postoperative IOP of 0-4 mmHg with a visibly shallow peripheral chamber on slit lamp
Constant watery discharge on a single eye after recent filtration surgery despite topical therapy
A new visual blur on day 2-5 after trabeculectomy, especially in patients with thin Tenon's tissue or mitomycin C exposure
emergencySigns: Sudden vision loss to hand motions or worse in a recently operated eye — may indicate kissing choroidal effusions or suprachoroidal hemorrhage
Severe pain with a hazy cornea and visible iris-cornea contact across the entire chamber (Spaeth grade 4) — corneal decompensation imminent
Purulent discharge with a flat bleb and hypopyon — possible blebitis or endophthalmitis, an ophthalmic emergency
Sudden onset of distorted central vision with new photopsias after trauma to the eye — may signal retinal detachment complicating hypotony

causes.description: The most common cause overall. A defect in the conjunctival closure or scleral flap allows aqueous to escape externally. Risk is highest in the first 24-72 hours and rises with thin conjunctiva, antimetabolite use (mitomycin C), and limbal incision technique. Seidel test under cobalt-blue light streams clear aqueous through fluorescein-stained tears.

Excessive transscleral aqueous flow through a thin, avascular, or cystic bleb. Seidel negative. Accounts for roughly 20-30% of post-trabeculectomy flat AC cases and is more common after mitomycin C 0.4 mg/mL applied for 3-5 minutes.

A separation of the ciliary body from the scleral spur creates a direct route from the AC into the suprachoroidal space. Causes include blunt ocular trauma and iatrogenic injury during cataract surgery (especially manual extracapsular techniques) or glaucoma drainage device placement.

Aqueous flows freely through a non-valved (Baerveldt, Molteno) tube before fibrous capsule formation around the plate (4-6 weeks), or through a valved Ahmed shunt with failed valve resistance. Causes shallow AC, choroidal effusion, and hypotony maculopathy.

causes.cause: Post-cataract wound dehiscence or iris incarceration
causes.description: Cataract incision dehiscence with vitreous prolapse or iris incarceration into the wound creates a persistent low-resistance leak. More common in eyes with thin sclera, high myopia, or prior radial keratotomy.

Chronic uveitis, retinal detachment with proliferative vitreoretinopathy, prolonged aqueous-suppressant therapy (timolol, dorzolamide, brimonidine combinations), or cycliotic membranes can reduce aqueous secretion to a point where the chamber shallows.

riskFactors.factor: Recent trabeculectomy or glaucoma drainage device surgery
riskFactors.description: The single largest risk factor. 4-15% of trabeculectomies develop early postoperative flat AC; risk is highest in the first 5 days and falls sharply once bleb fibrosis stabilizes.

Antimetabolites reduce bleb scarring and improve IOP control but increase the risk of thin avascular blebs that overfilter or leak. Mitomycin C 0.4 mg/mL for 3 minutes roughly doubles the rate of bleb-related complications compared with no antimetabolite.
Direct trauma can disrupt the ciliary body insertion at the scleral spur and create a cyclodialysis cleft. Sports injuries, fistfights, and airbag deployment are common sources.
Eyes with axial length over 26 mm or scleral thickness under 0.5 mm have reduced wound integrity and a higher rate of wound leak and overfiltration after intraocular surgery.
Short eyes (axial length under 20 mm) develop choroidal effusion and shallow AC more readily after even small IOP drops because of altered scleral compliance.
Thin conjunctiva and reduced Tenon's tissue increase risk of wound leak. Wound healing is also slower, prolonging the window of vulnerability.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic uveitis or anterior segment inflammation
riskFactors.description: Inflammation impairs ciliary body function and can cause both early and late hypotony. Prior pars planitis, juvenile idiopathic arthritis-associated uveitis, and Fuchs heterochromic iridocyclitis carry higher risk.

riskFactors.factor: Prior vitrectomy or complex retinal surgery
riskFactors.description: Eyes with prior vitreoretinal procedures have altered aqueous dynamics, scleral integrity, and a higher rate of postoperative hypotony when subsequent anterior segment surgery is performed.

affectedDemographics: Most cases occur in adults age 55-80 who have undergone glaucoma surgery, with men and women affected equally
African-Caribbean and African-American patients have higher trabeculectomy rates due to more aggressive glaucoma and therefore see proportionally more postoperative flat AC events
Children with congenital or juvenile glaucoma have a higher absolute rate of flat AC after trabeculectomy (up to 20%) because of thinner sclera and ocular hypotony tolerance differences
High myopes and patients with prior refractive or vitreoretinal surgery are disproportionately represented across all age groups
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of flat anterior chamber with hypotony combines slit lamp examination, Seidel testing, intraocular pressure measurement, gonioscopy, and imaging. The first step is slit-lamp grading of anterior chamber depth using the Spaeth system: grade 1 indicates a shallow peripheral AC, grade 2 shallow throughout, grade 3 iris-cornea touch with central AC preserved, and grade 4 complete lens-cornea apposition. The Seidel test is essential when post-surgical leak is suspected — fluorescein is applied to the operative site and viewed under cobalt-blue light; a streaming dilution of dye indicates aqueous escape. Intraocular pressure measurement is challenging at very low IOP because Goldmann applanation tonometry under-reads below 5 mmHg — a Tono-Pen or pneumotonometer gives more reliable values in this range. Gonioscopy through a fluid-filled four-mirror lens identifies cyclodialysis clefts as a visible angle recess with iridocorneal angle widening; ultrasound biomicroscopy (UBM) detects occult clefts and provides anatomic mapping for surgical planning. B-scan ultrasonography identifies choroidal effusion (kissing or non-kissing), retinal detachment, and choroidal hemorrhage. Dilated fundus exam looks for hypotony maculopathy — macular folds and optic disc edema from reduced posterior segment volume. Anterior segment OCT can document AC depth precisely and is useful in monitoring response to treatment. The differential includes pupillary block, malignant glaucoma (which has shallow AC with normal or high IOP, not hypotony), and suprachoroidal hemorrhage.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes severity of anterior chamber shallowing and identifies iris-cornea or lens-cornea touch that drives endothelial loss and cataract progression. Documents bleb morphology, conjunctival integrity, and corneal status.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless examination at the slit lamp lasting 5-10 minutes. The ophthalmologist uses a narrow light beam to grade the chamber from 0-4 and inspects the operative wound under high magnification.

diagnosticTests.test: Seidel test with fluorescein under cobalt-blue light
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects external wound leak by visualizing dilution of fluorescein-stained tear film by clear aqueous humor. A positive Seidel confirms wound leak as the mechanism and dictates immediate management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fluorescein strip is touched to the conjunctiva, and the operative site is examined under cobalt-blue illumination. Gentle pressure may be applied to provoke a leak. The test takes 2-3 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Intraocular pressure measurement with Tono-Pen or pneumotonometer
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the degree of hypotony. Goldmann applanation tonometry under-reads below 5 mmHg; Tono-Pen and pneumotonometer are more accurate in this range.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After topical anesthetic, the probe is briefly applied to the cornea. Each reading takes seconds; several readings are averaged. The procedure is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies cyclodialysis cleft as a visible recess between the ciliary body and scleral spur. May require gentle indentation to open the angle.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A goniolens is placed on the anesthetized cornea while the patient sits at the slit lamp. The examination takes 5-10 minutes and may cause mild pressure sensation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: High-frequency ultrasound that visualizes the ciliary body and angle structures. Detects occult cyclodialysis clefts not visible on gonioscopy and maps their extent for surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient lies supine; a saline-filled eye cup is placed on the anesthetized eye and a probe scans the anterior segment. The test takes 15-20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies posterior segment complications of hypotony — choroidal effusion (serous or hemorrhagic), retinal detachment, and vitreous opacities — especially when the view through a cloudy cornea or cataract is limited.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Coupling gel is applied to closed eyelids and a probe images the eye in multiple positions. The scan takes 10 minutes and is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-contact imaging that quantifies AC depth, angle width, and bleb morphology. Useful for serial monitoring during conservative management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient looks into a scanner for 1-2 minutes per eye. No contact with the eye, no dye, no discomfort.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of flat anterior chamber with hypotony is dictated by the underlying mechanism and the severity of chamber shallowing. The general principle is to restore aqueous balance — reduce outflow if it is excessive, increase production if it is depressed, or seal the leak if external. For Seidel-positive wound leak, first-line management is pressure patching with cycloplegia (atropine 1% twice daily), discontinuation of aqueous-suppressant drops, and topical antibiotic prophylaxis. A large-diameter bandage contact lens or a Simmons scleral shell tamponades the leak. Most fresh leaks (under 1 week) close within 2-5 days with this approach. Persistent or late leaks require surgical revision: conjunctival resuturing with 10-0 nylon or 9-0 vicryl, scleral flap re-suturing, or autologous blood injection into a thin overfiltering bleb to promote scarring. For overfiltering Seidel-negative blebs, a bandage contact lens, transconjunctival compression sutures, or bleb revision with conjunctival advancement are used progressively. For cyclodialysis clefts, first-line therapy is atropine 1% three times daily for 4-6 weeks to relax the ciliary body and promote re-apposition; persistent clefts are treated by argon laser photocoagulation (200-500 mW spots) along the cleft margin or by direct surgical cyclopexy with scleral sutures. Tube-shunt overdrainage is managed by tube ligation with a removable suture, tube stenting, or external compression. When kissing choroidal effusions occur, choroidal drainage with simultaneous AC reformation using viscoelastic restores anatomy and prevents permanent damage. Anterior chamber entry for cataract or IOL surgery is deferred until the AC is deep and stable. Throughout treatment, oral or topical corticosteroids may be needed to control associated inflammation, and aggressive lubrication protects the corneal endothelium from prolonged iris-cornea touch — endothelial cell loss accelerates after 7 days of contact.

medicalTreatments.name: Cycloplegic-mydriatic therapy (atropine 1% drops 2-3 times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Relaxes the ciliary body, deepens the anterior chamber, and breaks ciliochoroidal block. First-line for both flat AC after filtration surgery and cyclodialysis cleft. Continued for 2-6 weeks depending on mechanism.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: AC reformation in 60-70% of mild cases within 5-7 days when used alongside pressure patching or bandage contact lens.

medicalTreatments.name: Pressure patching and Simmons scleral shell
medicalTreatments.description: Mechanical tamponade of conjunctival or scleral wound leaks. Bandage contact lens (BCL) of 18-22 mm diameter or a Simmons shell is placed for 3-7 days. Aqueous-suppressant drops are discontinued during this period.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Fresh leaks under 7 days seal in 70-80% of cases. Late leaks have a lower success rate (40-50%) and often require surgical revision.

medicalTreatments.name: Autologous blood injection into bleb
medicalTreatments.description: 0.1-0.3 mL of the patient's own venous blood is injected into a thin overfiltering bleb to promote inflammation and scarring. Used for late leaks and chronic overfiltration. Risk of infection is low when performed under sterile conditions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IOP normalization in 50-70% of cases at 6 months; may require repeat injections.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate 1% 4-6 times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Control associated inflammation from surgery, trauma, or persistent iris-cornea touch. Tapered slowly over 4-8 weeks once AC reforms. Avoid in active wound leak as steroids delay epithelial healing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces inflammation-driven cataract progression and posterior synechiae formation in most patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Discontinuation of aqueous-suppressant therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Beta-blockers, alpha agonists, and carbonic anhydrase inhibitors all reduce aqueous production. Stopping these drops allows aqueous to rebuild the chamber. Standard first move when hypotony is identified.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest standalone effect; essential adjunct to mechanical and surgical measures.

medicalTreatments.name: Argon laser photocoagulation of cyclodialysis cleft
medicalTreatments.description: 200-500 mW spots, 100-200 micrometers, 0.1 second duration applied through a gonioscopy lens along the cleft margin to induce inflammation and apposition of the ciliary body to the scleral spur.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cleft closure in 60-75% of selected cases; may require multiple sessions and is more effective for short, focal clefts.

medicalTreatments.name: Viscoelastic injection into the anterior chamber
medicalTreatments.description: Cohesive viscoelastic (sodium hyaluronate 1-2%) is injected through a paracentesis to mechanically reform the AC. Used as a temporary measure or in conjunction with definitive surgery for kissing choroidals.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides immediate AC reformation; lasts 24-72 hours before viscoelastic washes out. Bridge therapy, not definitive.

surgicalOptions.name: Conjunctival or scleral flap re-suturing
surgicalOptions.description: Definitive repair of a persistent wound leak. Performed under microscope with 10-0 nylon or 9-0 vicryl. Tenon's tissue is dissected, mobilized, and advanced to cover the defect.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Leak resolution in 85-95% of cases; recurrent leak in 5-10% requires further revision.

surgicalOptions.name: Bleb revision with conjunctival advancement
surgicalOptions.description: For thin, leaking, or overfiltering blebs, the bleb is excised and fresh conjunctiva is advanced from adjacent areas to recreate a controlled filtration site. May be combined with antimetabolite reapplication.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic and IOP improvement in 70-85% of patients at 1 year.

surgicalOptions.name: Direct surgical cyclopexy
surgicalOptions.description: Open repair of a cyclodialysis cleft. The conjunctiva and sclera are opened over the cleft and the ciliary body is sutured back to the scleral spur with 10-0 nylon. Used when laser fails or the cleft is extensive.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cleft closure in 80-90% of cases; risk of postoperative IOP spike requires close monitoring.

surgicalOptions.name: Tube-shunt revision (ligature, stenting, repositioning)
surgicalOptions.description: For tube-shunt overdrainage. A removable 7-0 vicryl ligature around the tube, an internal stent, or repositioning of the tube into a smaller anterior chamber bite controls flow.
surgicalOptions.successRate: IOP normalization in 70-85% of cases at 6 months.

surgicalOptions.name: Choroidal drainage with AC reformation
surgicalOptions.description: When kissing choroidal effusions persist beyond 5-7 days, posterior sclerotomies drain serous fluid while the AC is reformed with viscoelastic or balanced salt solution. Often combined with bleb or wound repair in the same setting.
surgicalOptions.successRate: AC and choroidal anatomy restored in 80-90% of cases; vision recovery depends on duration of preceding hypotony.

alternativeTreatments.name: Aqueous augmentation with topical ibopamine 2%
alternativeTreatments.description: Investigational topical dopaminergic agonist that stimulates aqueous production. Used in some centers for chronic post-vitrectomy or post-traumatic hypotony where the wound is intact. Limited availability outside research settings.

alternativeTreatments.name: Compression sutures across the bleb (Palmberg sutures)
alternativeTreatments.description: Transconjunctival 9-0 nylon sutures placed across the bleb to flatten and consolidate it. Useful for overfiltering blebs that are not yet ready for revision.

whySeeSpecialist: Flat anterior chamber with hypotony is an ophthalmic urgency that requires evaluation by a glaucoma or anterior segment specialist within 24-48 hours of onset. Generalist ophthalmologists initiate first-line conservative measures and arrange immediate subspecialist referral. Patients should not wait for a routine appointment — corneal endothelial cell loss accelerates after 7 days of iris-cornea contact, peripheral anterior synechiae form rapidly, and untreated kissing choroidals can cause permanent vision loss. A glaucoma subspecialist coordinates assessment of bleb integrity, cyclodialysis, and tube function; a cornea specialist evaluates endothelial reserve when prolonged contact is suspected; a retina specialist is involved when significant choroidal effusion or hemorrhage is present.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for fellowship training in glaucoma or anterior segment surgery, hands-on experience with trabeculectomy bleb revision and cyclopexy, access to ultrasound biomicroscopy and anterior segment OCT, and same-day or next-day operating room availability. Ask whether the surgeon performs both laser and surgical management of cyclodialysis clefts and whether the practice has a corneal endothelial specialist available for cases with prolonged iris-cornea touch. Continuity with the original glaucoma surgeon is preferable when possible.
preventionStrategies: Use of titratable releasable sutures or laser suture lysis in trabeculectomy to avoid early overfiltration
Careful intraoperative scleral flap construction with adequate thickness (one-third to one-half scleral depth) to prevent both leak and overfiltration
Conjunctival closure with watertight 10-0 nylon or 9-0 vicryl mattress sutures and intraoperative Seidel testing before leaving the operating room
Judicious use of antimetabolites — lower mitomycin C concentrations (0.2 mg/mL) and shorter application times (2 minutes) in eyes at higher leak risk
Avoidance of aggressive postoperative aqueous suppression in the first 5 days after trabeculectomy unless IOP is clearly elevated
Protective eyewear during sports and high-risk activities to prevent traumatic cyclodialysis
lifestyleModifications: Avoid eye rubbing, heavy lifting (over 10 kg), and Valsalva maneuvers for at least 4 weeks after filtration surgery
Wear a rigid protective shield at night for the first 2 weeks postoperatively and during any contact-sport return
Use preservative-free lubricating drops 6-8 times daily during the early postoperative period to protect the corneal endothelium
Sleep with the head slightly elevated (15-20 degrees) for the first week to reduce orbital venous pressure and choroidal effusion risk
Avoid swimming pools and hot tubs for 4 weeks after surgery to reduce wound-leak infection risk
Stop smoking — nicotine impairs conjunctival healing and increases late bleb leak risk

recommended: Adequate protein intake (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) to support conjunctival wound healing
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, peppers, kiwi) for collagen synthesis at the surgical site
Vitamin A sources (carrots, sweet potato, leafy greens) to support corneal epithelial integrity
Adequate hydration (1.5-2 L water daily) to maintain tear film and corneal hydration
Omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish, flaxseed) for ocular surface health
avoid: Excessive caffeine (over 400 mg daily) which can transiently raise blood pressure and orbital congestion
Heavy alcohol consumption, which impairs healing and may interact with postoperative medications
High-sodium processed foods that can cause systemic fluid shifts in eyes with marginal scleral integrity

exerciseGuidelines: Avoid all weight-bearing exercise above 50% of normal intensity for the first 2 weeks after filtration surgery or AC reformation. Walking on level ground is permitted from day 1. Stationary cycling at low resistance can resume at week 3 if the bleb appears stable and the AC is deep. Avoid swimming, contact sports, and yoga inversions for 6 weeks. Resume normal activity gradually after the 6-week postoperative review, with protective eyewear if returning to sports with projectile risk.
dailyManagement: Apply prescribed drops on the exact schedule — set phone alarms; missed doses delay healing and may extend hypotony
Avoid eye rubbing entirely and use a protective shield at night to prevent inadvertent pressure
Monitor your bleb daily in a mirror under good light; report any sudden enlargement, transparency, or discharge
Maintain hand hygiene before any drop instillation and store bottles uncontaminated
Keep all follow-up appointments — the first 6 weeks require visits at day 1, 3, 7, 14, 28, and 42 at minimum
prognosis: Prognosis depends on the underlying mechanism, the duration of iris-cornea contact, and the timeliness of intervention. With prompt conservative and surgical management, 70-90% of flat anterior chambers with hypotony reform within 1-2 weeks and final visual acuity returns to within one Snellen line of the preoperative baseline. The major prognostic factors are duration of contact (endothelial cell loss accelerates beyond 7 days flat), associated kissing choroidal effusions (which raise risk of permanent maculopathy), and concurrent suprachoroidal hemorrhage (which has worse outcomes regardless of intervention). Cyclodialysis clefts have generally excellent prognosis once closed — IOP often rises briefly to 30-40 mmHg post-closure and requires temporary aqueous suppression. Late complications include cataract progression (40-60% within 2 years if there was lens-cornea touch), peripheral anterior synechiae, and chronic corneal endothelial dysfunction requiring eventual endothelial keratoplasty in roughly 5-10% of severe prolonged cases.
recoveryTimeline: Most fresh wound leaks seal within 2-5 days of pressure patching and cycloplegic therapy. AC depth typically reforms within 3-7 days once aqueous flow is balanced. Cyclodialysis clefts treated with atropine close within 4-6 weeks; laser-treated clefts close within 2-4 weeks. After surgical bleb revision or cyclopexy, AC depth is usually restored within 24-48 hours, with progressive visual improvement over 2-4 weeks. Corneal edema from temporary iris-cornea touch resolves over 2-6 weeks; permanent endothelial dysfunction is uncommon if contact was under 7 days. Full visual recovery typically requires 6-12 weeks following resolution of hypotony.
complications: Accelerated corneal endothelial cell loss leading to bullous keratopathy when iris-cornea touch exceeds 7 days, sometimes requiring future endothelial keratoplasty (DSAEK or DMEK)
Cataract progression with up to 60% of patients developing visually significant lens opacity within 2 years when lens-cornea apposition occurred
Peripheral anterior synechiae formation that closes the drainage angle and converts open-angle to angle-closure glaucoma after AC reformation
Hypotony maculopathy with chorioretinal folds and reduced central vision that may not fully reverse even after IOP normalization
Choroidal effusion (serous or kissing) and suprachoroidal hemorrhage, the latter carrying significant risk of permanent vision loss
Blebitis and endophthalmitis from external wound leak — risk roughly 1% per year for the lifetime of a leaking bleb

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — EyeWiki on Hypotony
supportResources.description: Peer-reviewed clinical reference written and maintained by ophthalmologists with up-to-date management protocols.

supportResources.name: Glaucoma Research Foundation — After Surgery
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on what to expect after glaucoma surgery, including signs that warrant urgent contact with the surgeon.

supportResources.name: American Glaucoma Society
supportResources.description: Professional society resources and a surgeon locator for patients seeking glaucoma subspecialist care.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Glaucoma is characterized by elevated intraocular pressure (or pressure-related optic nerve damage) and is the disease that flat AC most often complicates as a postsurgical event — not the same entity. In flat AC with hypotony, IOP is critically low (<6 mmHg), the chamber is shallow, and the goal is to raise rather than lower pressure.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cataracts
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cataract is lens opacification that progresses gradually with age and causes painless visual blur. Flat AC with hypotony causes sudden visual blur with low IOP and shallow chamber on examination; however, lens-cornea touch in flat AC can accelerate cataract formation, and the two often coexist after complicated intraocular surgery.

confusedWithConditions.name: Normal Pressure Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Normal pressure glaucoma involves progressive optic nerve damage and visual field loss despite IOP within the statistically normal range (10-21 mmHg). In flat AC with hypotony, IOP is well below 6 mmHg, the chamber is anatomically shallow on slit lamp, and the clinical picture is acute rather than chronic.

confusedWithConditions.name: Macular Degeneration
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Age-related macular degeneration causes gradual central vision loss from retinal pigment epithelium and photoreceptor changes. Hypotony maculopathy mimics this with central distortion but follows a recent surgical or traumatic event with characteristic chorioretinal folds and resolves partially with restoration of IOP.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Flat AC with hypotony most often occurs as an early complication of glaucoma filtration surgery (trabeculectomy or tube shunt).

relatedConditions.name: Cataracts
relatedConditions.relevance: Lens-cornea contact during prolonged flat AC accelerates cataract formation; combined cataract-glaucoma surgery is a setting for both conditions.

relatedConditions.name: Normal Pressure Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Patients managed for low-tension glaucoma occasionally undergo filtration surgery and are vulnerable to postoperative flat AC due to baseline low IOP targets.

relatedConditions.name: Macular Degeneration
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypotony maculopathy can resemble or coexist with macular degeneration in older patients undergoing glaucoma surgery.

faqs.question: What does flat anterior chamber with hypotony mean?
faqs.answer: It means the space between cornea and iris has collapsed so the iris touches or nearly touches the cornea, while eye pressure has fallen below 6 mmHg. It usually happens within days of glaucoma surgery and is treated urgently to prevent corneal damage.

faqs.question: How serious is flat anterior chamber after eye surgery?
faqs.answer: It is a sight-threatening urgency. Without treatment within days, the corneal inner cell layer (endothelium) is damaged by iris contact, cataract may progress, and the retina can develop folds that reduce central vision. With prompt treatment, vision recovers in 70-90% of patients.

faqs.question: What causes a flat anterior chamber?
faqs.answer: Most cases follow glaucoma surgery: wound leak (hole in the surgical site), an overfiltering bleb (too much drainage), or a cyclodialysis cleft (ciliary body separation from the scleral wall). Tube-shunt overdrainage and reduced aqueous production are less common.

faqs.question: How is a wound leak detected?
faqs.answer: The eye surgeon performs a Seidel test — fluorescein dye is applied to the operative site and examined under cobalt-blue light. A clear stream of aqueous humor running through the dye indicates a leak. The test takes a few minutes and is painless.

faqs.question: What is the Spaeth grading for flat anterior chamber?
faqs.answer: Spaeth grading runs 0 to 4. Grade 0 is normal, grade 1 is shallow at the periphery, grade 2 shallow throughout, grade 3 peripheral iris-cornea touch with central chamber preserved, and grade 4 complete lens-cornea touch. Grades 3-4 are considered flat.

faqs.question: What is a cyclodialysis cleft?
faqs.answer: A cyclodialysis cleft is a separation of the ciliary body from the wall of the eye at the scleral spur. It creates a direct route for aqueous fluid to drain into the suprachoroidal space, dramatically lowering eye pressure. It is diagnosed by gonioscopy or UBM.

faqs.question: How is a wound leak treated first?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is pressure patching, stopping aqueous-suppressant drops, and atropine 1% twice daily. A large bandage contact lens or a Simmons scleral shell may tamponade the leak. Most fresh leaks seal within 2-5 days.

faqs.question: What is an autologous blood injection for an overfiltering bleb?
faqs.answer: 0.1-0.3 mL of the patient's own venous blood is injected into a thin overfiltering bleb. The blood induces inflammation and scarring that thickens the bleb and reduces drainage. Used for late leaks and chronic overfiltration when conservative measures fail.

faqs.question: Can a flat anterior chamber damage the cornea permanently?
faqs.answer: Yes. When the iris touches the cornea beyond 7 days, the corneal endothelium loses cells faster than normal. Severe loss causes corneal clouding and may require endothelial transplant. Rapid chamber reformation prevents this damage.

faqs.question: What is hypotony maculopathy?
faqs.answer: Hypotony maculopathy is a complication in which sustained low eye pressure produces folds in the macula, the central retina. It causes distorted central vision and is detected on dilated exam or OCT. It usually improves when pressure normalizes but may leave residual blur.

faqs.question: Why are aqueous-suppressant drops stopped during treatment?
faqs.answer: Aqueous suppressants (beta-blockers, alpha agonists, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors) reduce the production of aqueous fluid inside the eye. When the chamber is already flat from too little fluid, these drops worsen the problem. Stopping them allows the eye to rebuild the chamber naturally.

faqs.question: How long does it take for the anterior chamber to reform?
faqs.answer: With proper treatment, most fresh wound leaks seal in 2-5 days and the chamber reforms in 3-7 days. Cyclodialysis clefts treated with atropine alone may take 4-6 weeks to close. After surgical revision or laser treatment, the chamber usually reforms within 24-48 hours.

faqs.question: Can flat anterior chamber happen without surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes, but it is uncommon. Blunt trauma can create a cyclodialysis cleft, and severe chronic uveitis or retinal detachment can shut down aqueous production enough to shallow the chamber. The vast majority of cases follow recent glaucoma or cataract surgery.

faqs.question: What is the Simmons scleral shell?
faqs.answer: A Simmons shell is a rigid scleral lens that covers the cornea and operative site of a leaking glaucoma bleb. It mechanically tamponades the leak and prevents external fluid loss while the conjunctiva heals. It is removed in clinic after 3-7 days, depending on response.

faqs.question: Why does my eye surgeon use Tono-Pen instead of the usual blue applanation tonometer?
faqs.answer: Goldmann applanation tonometry, the usual office device, is unreliable at very low pressures (below 5 mmHg) and may give a falsely positive reading. Tono-Pen and pneumotonometer give more accurate measurements in the hypotony range and are preferred for monitoring response to treatment.

faqs.question: What are kissing choroidal effusions?
faqs.answer: Kissing choroidals are large pockets of serous fluid that have collected under the choroid on opposite sides of the eye and have grown until they touch in the middle. They occur as a complication of prolonged low eye pressure and require urgent surgical drainage to prevent permanent damage.

faqs.question: Will I need another surgery if my chamber stays flat?
faqs.answer: Most flat anterior chambers resolve with conservative measures in 5-7 days. If the chamber stays flat or kissing choroidals develop, surgical revision (conjunctival resuturing, bleb revision, choroidal drainage) is performed. Roughly 30-40% of cases need revision.

faqs.question: Can I exercise after my chamber reforms?
faqs.answer: Avoid weight-bearing exercise and bending over for at least 2 weeks after chamber reformation. Walking is permitted from day 1. Stationary cycling at low intensity can resume at week 3. Contact sports, swimming, and yoga inversions should wait at least 6 weeks and only after surgeon clearance.

faqs.question: What is the long-term outcome after flat anterior chamber?
faqs.answer: With prompt treatment, 70-90% of patients regain vision within one Snellen line of preoperative baseline. Outcome depends on duration of iris-cornea contact, choroidal complications, and corneal endothelial reserve. Cataract progresses in 40-60% if lens-cornea touch occurred.

faqs.question: When should I contact my eye surgeon urgently after glaucoma surgery?
faqs.answer: Call your surgeon the same day if you notice sudden visual blur, increased watering, eye pain, a bleb that looks dramatically different, or any discharge from the operative site. Flat anterior chamber is most treatable when caught in the first 24-72 hours.

simpleName: Flat anterior chamber with low eye pressure after surgery
metaTitle: Flat Anterior Chamber with Hypotony | aihealz
metaDescription: Flat anterior chamber with hypotony is an ophthalmic urgency after glaucoma surgery or trauma. Learn the causes, signs, diagnosis, and reformation treatments.

images.altText: Slit lamp examination of the anterior segment, the primary tool for grading anterior chamber depth and diagnosing flat anterior chamber with hypotony
images.caption: Slit lamp biomicroscopy is the diagnostic cornerstone for grading anterior chamber depth (Spaeth 0-4) and detecting wound leaks.

images.altText: Trabeculectomy bleb on the superior conjunctiva, the surgical site most commonly associated with postoperative flat anterior chamber
images.caption: Trabeculectomy bleb visible on superior conjunctiva. Overfiltering or leaking blebs are a leading cause of flat anterior chamber with hypotony.
